narrative:
The market sits firmly in the business as usual quadrant, with a stress score of 21 out of 100 confirming a calm underlying environment. What keeps it there is the alignment between subdued credit spreads and a VIX that, while modestly elevated, has not broken into territory that signals systemic concern. The recent equity softness looks more like a routine pullback than the beginning of a dislocated regime.

VIX at 18.9 sits at the 52nd percentile of its ten-year range, meaning it is neither particularly cheap nor alarming, and the contango term structure reinforces that options markets are not pricing near-term crisis risk. High yield spreads at 277 basis points sit at just the 13th percentile of their five-year range, and investment grade at 76 basis points tells a similar story of historically tight credit conditions. Credit is clearly not confirming any equity stress and is in fact providing a counterweight to what equity price action alone might suggest.

The standout geographic and sector moves deserve attention despite the otherwise calm backdrop. EEM has fallen 6.2% over five days and EWZ is down 5.6%, pointing to concentrated pressure in emerging markets that runs ahead of the broader red count in that asset class. On the sector side, technology via XLK is off 5.9% over the same period, a meaningful divergence from defensive names like healthcare and real estate, which have gained ground and are acting as the market's ballast.
